one_line_takeaway: By pairing a source-level, structure-aware coverage tree (ECT) with an LLM-based Solve-Complete constraint solver and history-guided seed generation, COTTONTAIL raises syntactic validity of concolic-execution-generated test inputs by roughly two orders of magnitude over Z3, driving 30.73%/41.32% average line/branch coverage gains over SymCC and MARCO and surfacing 6 new CVEs in MuJS, QuickJS, and UnQLite.
key_finding: COTTONTAIL improves line coverage by 20.77%-38.16% (30.73% average, up to 99.65% on QuickJS) and branch coverage by 25.56%-57.70% (41.32% average, up to 175.88% on Libexpat) over the best baselines in Setting 1, covering up to 12k more lines and 9k more branches in total (Table 2). The LLM-driven solver raises parser-checking pass rates roughly 100x over Z3 on the four investigative-study libraries (e.g., Libxml2: 2.1%->46.01%; SQLite: 14.7%->62.69%; MuJS: 0.1%->22.74%; JSON-C: 12.46%->43.78%, Figure 1); a broader 8-library comparison (Figure 9) shows COTTONTAIL passing rates such as 32.61% on Libxml2 vs. SymCC's 4.74% and MARCO's 13.90%. The LLM-driven solver directly produces correct solutions in 70.08% of cases (29.92% failure, handled by the fallback validator). COTTONTAIL found 6 previously unknown memory-safety vulnerabilities (Table 4: 2 in MuJS, 2 in QuickJS, 2 in UnQLite -- memory leak, heap overflow x2, stack overflow x2, global overflow), all assigned CVEs (CVE-2024-55061, CVE-2025-26082, CVE-2024-13903, CVE-2025-26081, CVE-2025-26083, CVE-2025-3791), 4 of 6 fixed by the time of writing. MARCO detected only 1 of the 6 (spending 26.67% of its 12-hour budget on CSTG scheduling for MuJS); SymCC detected 4 of 6. In a case study, COTTONTAIL triggered a MuJS heap overflow via Ap_sort_cmp within 55 iterations vs. SymCC's 535 and SymCC(¬MAP)'s 1,811; MARCO produced 26,575 test inputs (99.9% invalid) and never found the trigger in 12 hours. Ablations: ECT-guided selection cuts collected path constraints sharply vs. NoMap/Bitmap while preserving or improving coverage (e.g., first phase JSON-C: 67 ECT vs. 223 NoMap vs. 106 Bitmap constraints, identical coverage; second phase JSON-C: 926 ECT vs. 7,024 NoMap). CoT (Solve-Complete) beats a no-CoT variant (COTTONTAIL(NormPro)) on line coverage across all 8 libraries (e.g., SQLite: ~13,000->~15,000+ lines). The test case validator improves or matches coverage over a no-validator variant in all libraries. History-guided seed generation beats random seeding (e.g., SQLite: COTTONTAIL >23,000 lines vs. RandSeed <19,000) and continues adding coverage after baselines saturate (e.g., +376 lines on SQLite, +806 on QuickJS over the last 10 of 12 hours). Estimated operating cost with gpt-4o-mini is reported at 0.78 USD/hour.
core_contribution: COTTONTAIL is an LLM-driven concolic execution engine, built on top of SymCC, for generating highly structured (syntactically valid) test inputs to fuzz parsing programs (XML, SQL, JavaScript, JSON). It contributes three new components: (1) Structure-aware Constraint Selection, using a new coverage representation called the Expressive Coverage Tree (ECT) built from source-level structural instrumentation (branch info collector) to select non-redundant, structure-aware path constraints via a two-phase reduction and a node-weight scoring function (Nodeweight = alpha*untaken + beta*visit_cnt + gamma*depth, with alpha=1.0, beta=3.0, gamma=0.8); (2) LLM-driven Constraint Solving via a 'Solve-Complete' Chain-of-Thought paradigm, where the LLM first solves a Constraint Mask ([k!n]) for satisfiability/syntax validity and then completes a Flexible Mask ([xxx]) to produce syntactically valid, flexibly-sized test inputs, paired with a Test Case Validator (Algorithm 1) that falls back to a traditional solver's solution when the LLM's output fails to satisfy the constraint; (3) History-guided Seed Acquisition, which prompts an LLM (with CoT) to generate initial seeds before testing and fresh seeds when coverage saturates (no increase for 3 minutes), using a history coverage recorder that maps prior test inputs to covered/uncovered branches.
threat_model: Not an attacker/defender threat model in the adversarial-ML sense; this is an offensive-security test-generation tool. The 'threat' addressed is that of latent memory-safety vulnerabilities (heap/stack overflows, memory leaks) in widely used open-source parsing libraries that existing white-box fuzzers (concolic executors) fail to find because they produce mostly syntactically invalid inputs that never reach deep application logic. The paper's own investigative study found that solving SymCC-collected path constraints with Z3 yields 85.3%-99.9% syntactically invalid test cases across Libxml2, SQLite, MuJS, and JSON-C, motivating the LLM-driven solver.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations/threats: (1) COTTONTAIL's effectiveness depends on the completeness/quality of the fuzzing driver, which is itself hard to write; (2) as a source-code-based (not binary-based) concolic executor, it requires access to source code, and the current implementation targets C/C++-compiled test programs (the paper notes an unaddressed direction for binary-only targets); (3) external validity: only SymCC and MARCO (and their variants) were used as baselines, and only 8 libraries across 4 formats were evaluated -- the authors explicitly do not claim generalization to large software systems such as V8 or GCC, citing scalability limits common to concolic execution; (4) possible LLM data leakage/memorization is acknowledged as a threat but the authors argue it is unlikely because the constraint-solving process/prompt format is unconventional; (5) LLM hallucination is a known internal-validity threat, mitigated (per the authors) by the Test Case Validator fallback to a traditional solver, and by 5x-repeated runs to reduce randomness; (6) LLM response time/robustness is flagged by the authors as an area needing further improvement despite being 'relatively low cost.'

datasets_used: Seed corpus reused from the MARCO paper for fair comparison
quantitative_results: Line coverage improvement: 20.77%-38.16% range, 30.73% average, up to 99.65% (QuickJS)
Branch coverage improvement: 25.56%-57.70% range, 41.32% average, up to 175.88% (Libexpat)
Total coverage gain: +12k lines, +9k branches vs. best baselines
Parser-checking pass-rate gain vs. Z3 (investigative study, Figure 1): Libxml2 2.1%->46.01%; SQLite 14.7%->62.69%; MuJS 0.1%->22.74%; JSON-C 12.46%->43.78% (>100x improvement overall)
LLM solver direct success rate: 70.08% (failure rate 29.92%, covered by fallback validator)
MARCO detected only 1/6 new vulnerabilities; SymCC detected 4/6
Case-study trigger speed: COTTONTAIL 55 iterations vs. SymCC 535 vs. SymCC(¬MAP) 1,811 vs. MARCO (failed in 12 hours, 26,575 test cases, 99.9% invalid)
ECT vs. NoMap/Bitmap path-constraint counts (first phase): JSON-C 67 vs. 223 vs. 106; SQLite 137 vs. 401 vs. 191
History-guided seeding vs. random seeding on SQLite: >23,000 lines vs. <19,000 lines over 12 hours
Estimated LLM operating cost: 0.78 USD/hour using gpt-4o-mini
baselines_compared: SymCC(¬MAP) (selects all newly generated path constraints)
Traditional Z3 solver (compared against the LLM-driven solver in the investigative study)
Ablation self-baselines: COTTONTAIL(NormPro) (no CoT), COTTONTAIL(¬Val) (no test case validator), COTTONTAIL(RandSeed), COTTONTAIL(Init+¬SGen), COTTONTAIL(Init+SGen)
